---
title: "Multivariate modeling of nutrient-flow mechanisms and yield formation"
subtitle: "Project overview and presentation draft"
format:
  pptx:
    slide-level: 2
---

# Project motivation

## Dataset

- Fertilizer composition variables
- Soil nutrient measurements
- Plant nutrient measurements
- Grain nutrient measurements
- Weather variables
- Biomass and harvest indicators
- Final grain yield

## Main challenge

- Small number of observations
- Many correlated variables
- Strong mechanistic dependencies

---

# Scientific background

## Nutrient-flow DAG

```text
fertilizer -> soil -> plant -> grain/yield
```

Additional interactions:

- phosphate availability
- drought stress
- heat stress
- biomass formation
- harvest allocation

## Main themes

- multicollinearity
- nonlinear effects
- prediction vs causality
- inferential limits

---

# Main scientific questions

## Q1 — Yield prediction

Which variables best predict grain yield?

Models:

- Linear regression
- Ridge regression
- Lasso regression
- Random Forest

Focus:

- prediction performance
- feature stability
- nonlinear effects

---

# Main scientific questions

## Q2 — Fertilizer similarity

Do fertilizers produce distinguishable effects on grain yield?

Main goals:

- compare fertilizer effects
- evaluate uncertainty
- investigate overlap of effects

---

# Main scientific questions

## Q3 — Phosphate-dependent nitrogen uptake

Does soil nitrogen translate into plant nitrogen more strongly when phosphate availability is sufficient?

Main approaches:

- linear interaction models
- Random Forest dependency plots

---

# Main scientific questions

## Q4 — Drought-dependent nutrient uptake

Does drought weaken nutrient uptake or nutrient conversion?

Main focus:

- drought–nutrient interactions
- nonlinear weather effects

---

# Methodological philosophy

| Goal | Methods |
|---|---|
| Exploratory structure | Correlation analysis, PCA |
| Fertilizer comparison | Linear models |
| Prediction | Ridge, Lasso, RF |
| Mechanism exploration | Interaction models + RF |
| Causal interpretation | DAG reasoning |
| Honest evaluation | Cross-validation |

---

# Notebook 1 — EDA and multivariate structure

## Goals

- understand data structure
- explore fertilizer/year variability
- detect multicollinearity
- identify nonlinear patterns

## Analyses

- missingness
- yield distributions
- correlation heatmaps
- scatterplots
- PCA

---

# Notebook 1 — Expected outcomes

## Main findings

- strong nutrient correlation blocks
- evidence for multicollinearity
- possible nonlinear effects
- fertilizer overlap

## Impact downstream

- motivates Ridge/Lasso
- motivates RF
- informs causal interpretation

---

# Notebook 2 — Fertilizer similarity and data limits

## Goals

Determine whether fertilizers are distinguishable.

## Analyses

- yield ~ fertilizer
- year-adjusted models
- weather-adjusted models
- pairwise contrasts

---

# Notebook 2 — Main interpretation

Expected themes:

- overlapping fertilizer effects
- uncertainty dominates some contrasts
- weather/year effects may exceed fertilizer effects

---

# Notebook 3 — Prediction and Random Forest

## Main models

- Linear regression
- Ridge regression
- Lasso regression
- Random Forest

## Validation

- LOOCV or repeated k-fold CV

---

# Notebook 3 — Random Forest role

## Why RF?

Expected nonlinearities:

- drought thresholds
- nutrient saturation
- interactions

## RF outputs

- variable importance
- dependency plots
- interaction surfaces

---

# Notebook 4 — Causal DAG mechanisms

## Main mechanistic question

Does phosphate availability modify nitrogen uptake?

Main models:

```text
plant_N ~ soil_N * soil_phosphate + weather + year
```

and Random Forest analogues.

---

# Notebook 4 — Secondary mechanistic question

## Drought-dependent uptake

Example model:

```text
plant_N ~ soil_N * dryMay + weather + year
```

Goal:

- understand weather-modified uptake

---

# Causal interpretation strategy

## Main principles

- distinguish confounders from mediators
- prediction ≠ causality
- RF dependency plots are associational
- DAG guides interpretation

---

# Expected limitations

## Statistical limitations

- small sample size
- strong multicollinearity
- observational data
- only five years

## Consequences

- coefficient instability
- uncertain feature selection
- limited causal identification

---

# Expected contributions

## Scientific contribution

The project combines:

- multivariate exploratory analysis
- shrinkage methods
- nonlinear ML
- DAG-guided mechanism exploration
- explicit discussion of inferential limits

## Central message

Understanding where the dataset supports reliable conclusions is itself part of the scientific contribution.
